description: A 'wethaki' is an absorbent cloth used to wipe moisture from a body, surface, or object. It can dry hands beside a basin or wrap washed food before storage. 'wetha' (cloth) is the broader material; a towel is a piece kept ready to receive water.
articulatory_notes: Round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for voiced 'w', then move into true-mid front 'e'. Place the tongue tip lightly against the upper teeth for unvoiced 'th' and open into stressed central 'a'. Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into final high-front 'i'.
sound_symbolism: Rounded 'we' enters the broad breath of stressed 'tha' and keeps 'wetha' (cloth) whole. Final 'ki' closes at the back of the mouth and lifts into a bright edge. The open cloth seems to gather towards one finished corner.
usage_notes: Towels are countable. Size, material, purpose, and owner fit ordinary modifiers. The towel remains itself whether wet or dry; 'wosanu' (absorb) and 'wirua' (wet) state those conditions.

examples.phi: mia roe wethaki ha palu hewe.
examples.translation: I wipe this plate with a towel.

pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: A towel was washed and wrung, then dried and patched before it returned to its peg. The round continued until the cloth could take no more repair.

semantic_domains.activity: cloth used while wiping or drying
semantic_domains.physical: an absorbent textile kept to receive moisture
